coredata.dc:title: Carbon nanotubes, nanochains and quantum dots synthesized through the chemical treatment of charcoal powder
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, Sumeet
Das, Jayanta
coredata.dc:description: Here, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), carbon nanochains (CNCs) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized using charcoal powder in a mixture of concentrated H2SO4/HNO3 acidic solvent with the help of NaOH. It is demonstrated that the lower alkaline or basic environment is favourable to enhance the effectiveness for the formation of CNTs and CNCs whereas the higher alkaline or basic environment is effective for the formation of CQDs. The Raman measurements confirm the graphitic nature of the samples and also approve the structural changes in charcoal powder upon chemical treatment. The TEM measurement provides an evidence for the formation of CNTs, CNCs and CQDs from charcoal power as a result of varying pH of the reaction medium. The FTIR and XPS results further supports the chemical modification of charcoal powder by approving the appropriate bonding composition. The optical absorption behavior of the prepared samples was carried out with the help of UV–Vis. absorption spectra. The TG analysis shows the effect of chemical treatment on the thermal stability of the samples. Moreover, we have tried to provide an explanation to the experimental observations. This study not only provides insightful direction to synthesize CNTs, CNCs and CQDs in a facile way but also could lead to new developments in charcoal chemistries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Synthesis, Charcoal powder
Carbon nanostructures
Structural property
Thermal stability

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CNTs, CNCs and CQDs from charcoal powder
Mechanism for the formation of CNTs, CNCs and CQDs from charcoal powder

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: SK is thankful to Science & Engineering Research Board-DST, Govt. of India (File No. PDF/2017/000900) for providing financial support through National Postdoctoral Fellowship (NPDF) scheme.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for the synthesis procedure of CNTs, CNCs and CQDs.
Raman spectra of charcoal powder (C0) and the synthesized samples (C1, C2 and C3), respectively.
TEM images of C0 (a-c), C1(d-f), C2 (g-i) and C3(j-l). Inset of Fig. 3c, 3e, 3h, 3k: corresponding SAED patterns.
FTIR spectra of charcoal power (C0) and the synthesized samples (C1), (C2) and (C3).
XPS survey spectra of (a-c) C1 and (d-f) C2 samples.
TG curves of samples C0, C1, C2 and C3 in air atmosphere.

row.entry: Obtained from charcoal powder at pH7
Obtained from charcoal powder at pH9
Obtained from charcoal powder at pH11

head.ce:title: Carbon nanotubes, nanochains and quantum dots synthesized through the chemical treatment of charcoal powder

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Metallurgical and Materials Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur 721302 West Bengal, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Metallurgical and Materials Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Metallurgical and Materials Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur 721302 West Bengal, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), carbon nanochains (CNCs) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized using charcoal powder in a mixture of concentrated H2SO4/HNO3 acidic solvent with the help of NaOH. It is demonstrated that the lower alkaline or basic environment is favourable to enhance the effectiveness for the formation of CNTs and CNCs whereas the higher alkaline or basic environment is effective for the formation of CQDs. The Raman measurements confirm the graphitic nature of the samples and also approve the structural changes in charcoal powder upon chemical treatment. The TEM measurement provides an evidence for the formation of CNTs, CNCs and CQDs from charcoal power as a result of varying pH of the reaction medium. The FTIR and XPS results further supports the chemical modification of charcoal powder by approving the appropriate bonding composition. The optical absorption behavior of the prepared samples was carried out with the help of UV–Vis. absorption spectra. The TG analysis shows the effect of chemical treatment on the thermal stability of the samples. Moreover, we have tried to provide an explanation to the experimental observations. This study not only provides insightful direction to synthesize CNTs, CNCs and CQDs in a facile way but also could lead to new developments in charcoal chemistries.

ce:section.ce:para: The carbon nanostructures possess unique physical and chemical properties which can greatly contribute to the advancement of various state-of-the-art applications, including optoelectronic, bioimaging, solar cell, supercapacitor and so on [1–5]. There has been continuously an ongoing desire to make different carbon nanostructures like carbon nanotubes (CNTs), carbon nanoonions (CNOs), carbon nanofibers (CNFs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) through a cost effective way [6]. So far, graphite has been used as core raw materials of carbon source for synthesizing carbon nanostructures [7–9]. But the time consumption complex reaction process and high cost of raw materials have forced researchers to find out other alternatives which could really be beneficial for less expensive industrial scale production of carbon nanostructures. In the past few years, numerous studies have highlighted coal as one of the cheapest raw materials of carbon sources to synthesize carbon nanostructures such as CNTs, GQDs, CQDs and graphene [10–16]. After careful survey of the above-mentioned articles, it is reflected that carbon nanostructures were synthesized from coal with the help of either catalyst such as singlet transition metal particles (Fe), mixture of two different transition metal particles (Ni/Y, Zr/Ni and Ni–Sm2O3 or special environment such as ice water bath, oil bath, H2 atmosphere and mixture of H2/Ar and N2/Ar atmosphere. No one has claimed the synthesis of carbon nanostructures without the help of any catalyst or special environment.

In this context, the present work has aimed to synthesize different carbon nanostructures such as CNTs, CNCs and CQDs using charcoal powder. As the carbon nanostructures has gained the massive attention for various applications including optoelectronics, energy storage and bioimaging, there will obviously be a demand for producing it at low cost. The use of charcoal powder makes the present synthesis approach cost effective. Besides, the authors wanted to explore the possibility of charcoal powder rather than burning. The nomenclature for the charcoal powder and samples synthesized after chemical treatment of charcoal powder in this work is shown in Table 1 which is the following: C0-charcoal powder, C1-prepared at pH7, C2-prepared at pH9 and C3-prepared at pH11. The results obtained through Raman, TEM, FTIR, XPS and TGA measurements were carefully analysed and an attempt has been made to explain the formation of CNTs, CNCs and CQDs from charcoal powder.
The following materials were purchased from Merck, India and used without any purification:
Charcoal, sulphuric acid (H2SO4), nitric acid (HNO3), sodium hydroxide (NaOH) solution. The De-Ionized water was used in entire experiment for making ice and washing purposes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CNTs, CNCs and CQDs from charcoal powder
ce:section.ce:para: The schematic diagram of the synthesis procedure developed for CNTs, CNCs and CQDs is shown in Fig. 1 by taking advantage from previous report [11]. First of all, a water free acidic solvent was prepared using a mixture of 3:1 ratio of concentrated H2SO4 and HNO3 in a conical flask. Then, 300 mg of charcoal powder (obtained after grinding raw charcoal) was dispersed in this solvent through ultrasonic probe sonication at room temperature. The obtained homogeneous mixed solution of concentrated H2SO4/HNO3 and charcoal powder was then stirred using Teflon-lined magnetic bar and heated at 100 °C for 24 h at magnetic stirrer. Thereafter, the chemically treated solution of charcoal powder was cooled to room temperature and ice of 100 ml DI water was poured onto it. Instantly, NaOH solution of 3 M was added until the pH was reached at desired value. The resultant solution was then filtered and dried for 12 h at 60 °C to obtain CNTs, CNCs and CQDs in powder form. The yield of the synthesized CNTs and CNCs was found to be half of the mass of precursor (charcoal powder) used whereas the yield of CQDs was found to be one third of the mass of precursor (charcoal powder) used.

The structural information was obtained from micro-Raman spectrometer (Renishaw Basis Series, operating at a laser wavelength of 514 nm with output power 15 mW) measurements. The size, shape and morphology of the samples were investigated through TEM of TECNAI G2 20 S-TWIN operated at 200KV. The chemical structure was identified using FTIR spectrometer (Nicolet IMPACT 410) in transmission mode with a resolution of 4 cm−1 for each measurement over the spectral range of 400–4000 cm−1. XPS measurements were also carried out to investigate the chemical compositions of the synthesized samples via a PHI 5000 Verse Probe II system using a micro-focused monochromatic Al-Kα source (hυ=1486.6 eV). The thermal stability was measured using Perkin Elmer Pyris Diamond TG-DTA over the temperature range of 0–700 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C/min in air atmosphere.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for the formation of CNTs, CNCs and CQDs from charcoal powder
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, charcoal powder was used as a precursor of carbon source. It is confirmed from obtained experimental results, the charcoal powder consists of graphene nanosheets which has the characteristic C = C/C–C bonds of carbon atoms that can provide a basic framework to form carbon nanostructures. The present synthesis approach could be understood as:

First of all, raw charcoal was converted into charcoal powder through grinding. The prepared charcoal powder was then treated with acidic solvent of concentrated H2SO4/HNO3 mixture which attacks the C = C/CC carbon network of charcoal powder and causes breaking and shortening of graphitic structure to different extents [17]. This also facilitates the functionalization of graphene nanosheets of charcoal powder through the attachment of oxygen containing functional groups. During H2SO4/HNO3 treatment, one of the important roles of sulfuric acid is to penetrate charcoal powder through intercalation of HSO−4 molecules between their graphene sheets [18]. The HNO3 generates a large number of functional groups such as carbonyl, carboxyl, and nitrate groups which are attached with the surface of graphene sheets of charcoal powder [19]. After that, the acid treated graphene nanosheets of charcoal powder were exposed to ice, which involves hydrolysis of covalent sulfates and loss of all interlayer connection [20]. In the subsequent step, NaOH solution was added to the prepared mixture of charcoal powder and H2SO4/HNO3 which causes an increase in the content of hydroxyl groups and as a result, creates active sites which disturb carbon network of graphene structure [19]. As graphene nanosheets become smaller due to decomposition of the graphene layers, the effective periphery area becomes higher which increases the tendency of graphene layers to scroll up forming 0D or 1D carbon nanostructures in order to decrease the peripheral dangling bonds and thus the total energy of the system [21]. With increasing NaOH concentration, pH becomes more basic and increases negative charge on the graphene sheets and as a result, a repulsion is produced between the individual graphene layers which prevents the tendency of agglomeration of graphene sheets [22]. Despite the structural modifications of the graphene nanosheets of charcoal powder, the NaOH solution also reduces oxygen-containing functional groups simultaneously, preserving sp2 bonding of carbon network. [23]. Thus, both the acid mixture of H2SO4/HNO3 and NaOH solution lead to change in carbon structure of charcoal powder. The structural modifications of charcoal powder upon chemical treatment have been determined by Raman, UV–Vis., FTIR and XPS measurements. The TEM images have been used to determine the effect of present synthesis strategy on the formation of the prepared carbon nanostructures. The present analysis is perfectly in line with the structural modifications in coal reported previously [24–27].

Raman spectroscopy is particularly very sensitive technique for confirming graphitic nature of carbon-based materials [10]. The Raman spectra of the prepared samples (C0, C1, C2 and C3) recorded at room temperature are shown in Fig. 2 . The Raman spectra of all the samples display two fundamental D (sp3 carbon) and G (sp2 carbon) bands in the range of 1300–1700 cm−1. The D band is originated from the zone boundary phonons due to A1g symmetry and attributed to defects or disruption of translational symmetry in graphite lattice [28]. On the other hand, the G band is first order scattering corresponds to E2g symmetry and related to Brillouin zone center phonons of crystalline sp2 lattices in graphite [28]. However, unlike D and G bands, the 2D band in the Raman spectra of all the samples was not distinct. Thus, Raman measurements authenticate that the charcoal powder as well as the synthesized samples are graphitic carbon-based materials.

The value of Raman band positions of all the samples are given in Table 2 which reflects that the chemical treatment has intensively affected the graphitic structure of charcoal powder. The intensity and position of both the G and the D bands have undergone significant changes. The position of D and G bands are shifted towards higher or lower wavenumber with increasing pH value, indicating the changes in the fraction of sp3 carbons due to oxygen containing functional groups introduced upon chemical treatment [29]. Hence, the shifting in Raman band positions might be related to the stress generated in the graphitic structure of charcoal powder due to disorder produced to different extents [30–31]. In addition, it could also be noticed that the width of G band broadened in case of C1, C2 and C3 compared to C0. It is reported that the cutting of graphene sheets leads to broadening of G band [32].

It is known that the ratio of d-band intensity to G-band intensity (ID/IG) is a good index for measuring the quality of the synthesized carbon nanostructures. The value of ID/IG ratio for all the samples is shown in Table 2 . It is worth to note that in case of highly ordered graphitic materials, the expected ID/IG value turns out to be negligible because of the absence of D peaks but becomes prominent in highly defected graphitic materials [33]. The ID/IG ratio is known as ratio of amorphous/disordered carbon (sp3) to graphic carbon (sp2). The lower value of ID/IG of C0 compared to that of C1, C2 and C3 suggests that there is more graphic carbon (sp2) in charcoal powder in comparison to the synthesized samples. The ID/IG ratio of the synthesized materials is in order of ID/IG (C1)> ID/IG (C2)> ID/IG (C3) which suggests that sample C1 prepared at pH 7 has larger defect density while sample C3 prepared at pH 11 has lowest defect density. It also reflects that the content of armchair edges with respect to zigzag edges is higher in sample C1 as compared to samples C2 and C3 because the increased ID dominates the armchair edges [4]. Thus, the edge sites could be controlled by varying pH value. Also, the lowering of ID/IG ratio in sample C3 is the direct consequence of the enhanced conversion of C atoms from sp3 to sp2 [24]. Hence, the Raman results indicate the gradual improvement in sp2 hybridized carbon atom structure of the synthesized samples with increasing pH value. In order to support the above results, the average crystallite size of the sp2 domains (La ) was calculated using the formula [34]: La (nm)=
2.4
×
10
− 10
λ 4
(
I D
I G
)
− 1
where λ denotes the wavelength of the laser light in nm. The increase in the value of La (see Table 2) after increasing pH value is a direct indicative of the restoration of the sp2 moieties with pH value. Further, the distance between defects, LD (nm) was calculated using the formula
L D
(
n m
)
=
C ( λ ) / (
I D / I G
)
, being C(λ) = 102 nm2 [35]. The value of LD
is decreased with increasing pH value (see Table 1).

Also, the defect density (nD ) in the synthesized samples was calculated using the relation [36]:
N D
(
c
m
− 2
)
= 2
.4
×
10 22
λ
− 4
(
I D
I G
)
where λ denotes the wavelength of the laser light in nm . The defect density was found to be decreased with increasing pH from 7 to 11 (see Table 2). These results now indicate that the size of sp2 domain increases with decreasing defect density. This is also consistent with the calculated value of ID/IG. Basically, La depends on the amount of disorder caused by line or edge defects existing with the border area of graphitic domain while LD describes the density of point defects within graphitic domain [37]. The observed results then suggest that the D band not only comes from edges of graphene but also from structural disorder inside the graphene plane. Although, Raman analysis confirmed the graphitic nature of charcoal powder (C0) aa well as synthesized materials (C1, C2 and C3), it is difficult to distinguish that the synthesized materials are either GO or rGO or other derivatives of graphene (CNTs, CNCs etc.) because the Raman spectra of GO, rGO and other graphene derivatives have almost similar nature. Therefore, the synthesized materials are forwarded to TEM measurements.

In order to get size, shape and morphology of the charcoal powder (C0) as well as synthesized samples C1, C2 and C3, the TEM measurements were done. The TEM images of C0 shown in Fig. 3 a-c display the two dimensional (2D) folded and silky nature graphene nanosheets of hundreds of nanometres in lateral dimensions with localized carbon nanoparticles attached to the surface. The corresponding SAED pattern (see inset of Fig. 3c) of C0 shows diffuse ring without any bright spots. The TEM images of sample C1 shown in Fig. 3d-f at lower and higher magnification confirm the formation of CNTs. It could be observed from Fig. 3e that as-synthesized CNTs is multiwall carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) as the side graphitic wall fringes are clearly present. The diameter of as-synthesized MWCNTs is found to be ~20 nm with several nanometer long inner channel. A representative SAED pattern of as-synthesized MWCNTs has also been recorded and displays diffuse ring as shown in the inset of Fig. 3e. Further, the TEM images of sample C2 shown in Fig. 3g-i confirm the formation of CNCs. It is very clear from Fig. 3h that the interconnected carbon nanoparticles are arranged in such a way that they tend to form chain like 1D structure. The SAED pattern of synthesized CNCs shown in the inset of Fig. 3h exhibits randomly oriented sharp spots, indicating the interconnected carbon nanoparticles are oriented in different directions. The average diameter of as-prepared CNCs evaluated from TEM images is found to be ~15 nm. In comparison, the TEM images of the synthesized sample C3 shown in Fig. 3j-l confirms its dot like shape. Now, the question comes into our mind that although the synthesized sample C3 shows its dot like shape but what it is actually, either GQDs or CQDs. Prior to distinguish the dot like shape is either GQDs or CQDs, the SAED pattern of sample C3 should be discussed. The SAED pattern (see the inset of Fig. 3k) of CQDs shows the combination of diffuse and bright spots which are arranged randomly in a quasi-hexagonal type ring, indicating the non-even growth of graphene layers in all possible directions [37]. The TEM images and corresponding SAED pattern confirm that CQDs possess spherical shape and are composed of randomly oriented graphene layers. Hence, it is concluded that the synthesized sample C3 could be CQDs. Generally, the shape of CQDs reported so far approves their spherical nature [38]. The present SAED pattern of CQDs is consistent with that of previous reports [39–40]. The average size of the CQDs was calculated to be ~8 nm using scale given in TEM images. Hence, it is authenticated that the present synthesis strategy is favourable for preparing good quality CNTs, CNCs and CQDs in a cost-effective way.

While it is now confirmed the structural and morphological features of the synthesized samples, it is also essential to identify the presence of various functional groups and their bonding composition. Fig. 4 shows the FTIR spectra of charcoal powder (C0), depicting various absorption bands corresponding to different functional groups. The weaker absorption bands at ~ 874 and ~ 1429 cm–1 correspond to aromatic C = C stretching [11]. The another absorption band at ~ 1124 is also associated with in-plane C = C stretching [21]. This peak could also be ascribed to COC groups [41]. A broad and weaker band appeared at 3409 cm–1 related to the OH stretching was also detected, indicating a smaller content of hydroxyl groups in charcoal powder. The weaker absorption bands related to different functional groups shows that the carbon symmetry of the charcoal powder has less lattice distortions and defects [11].

However, the FTIR spectra (Fig. 4) of as-synthesized samples C1, C2 and C3 show a dramatic change compared to C0 which confirms the functionalization of surface structure of charcoal powder upon chemical treatment. It is obvious from FTIR spectra of as-synthesized samples that some new peaks are emerged while some existing peaks are either disappeared or shifted towards lower or higher wavenumber depending synthesis conditions. The absorption peaks arising at 619, 616 and 610 cm–1 in C1, C2 and C3, respectively corresponds to OH out-of-plane bending vibrations [42]. This peak is absent in C0 but prominent in C1 and C2 compared to C3 which shows that OH out-of-plane bending vibrations become prominent at lower pH value in comparison to higher one. The peaks observed at 1128, 1111 and 1099 cm–1 represent C–O stretching vibrations of alkoxy/epoxy in C1, C2 and C3, respectively [43]. Furthermore, the peaks appeared near about ~ 1125 cm–1 corresponds to in-plane C = C skeleton vibrations [21]. Besides, the peak near about ~ 1100 cm–1 is also originated from the skeleton vibrations of C–C [44]. Moreover, the peaks obtained at 1627, 1579 and 1559 cm–1 in C1, C2 and C3, respectively, are referred to the characteristics C = C stretching bond of sp2 hybridized graphitic carbon atom network [45]. The broad peaks found at 3436, 3412, 3395 cm–1 in C1, C2 and C3, respectively, correspond to the stretching vibrations of OH bonds and it is sharper in C1compared to C2 and then C3, indicating a larger content of hydroxyl groups in an order of C1>C2>C3. It is also clear that the OH stretching vibration bond is shifted towards lower wavenumber side in the order of C1>C2>C3 which indicates that the strong inter-molecular hydrogen bonding is developed with increasing pH value in as-synthesized samples compared to charcoal powder [46]. The peak at 2916 cm–1 are emerged in C2 and C3 which is assigned to –CH3, indicating small amount of functionalized hydrogen moieties with carbon network [10]. The small peaks at 1209, 1339, 1710 cm–1 in C3 indicates the presence of C–O, C–H and carbonyl (C = O) groups present in carboxylic groups (-COOH) compared to other samples [11,41,43]. The minor peak at 1193 cm–1 in C1 represents COC (epoxy) groups and at 1381 cm–1 in C1 and C2 corresponds to methyl (–CH3) [10]. All of these findings confirm the generation and removal of oxygen functionalities during chemical treatment of charcoal powder.

It is also observed that transmission reduces to ~ 20% in C1 compared to ~ 55% of C0, and further, increases up to 50% in C2 and 65% in C3 with increasing pH value, confirming the varying composition of carbon, oxygen and hydrogen through chemical treatment. Also, the prominent peaks are shifted towards lower wavenumber while the pH value is increased from 7 to 11 which indicates the enhancement of the excitonic features due to increased electron–hole interactions in the synthesized carbon nanostructures. It is reported that the reduced dimensionality increases the electron–hole interaction due to the strong quantum confinement which enhances excitonic effect. The reduced dimensionality restricts the movement of electron which modifies the nature of exciton and leads to the distinguished optical behavior [47]. Thus, the FTIR results reveal the surface and edge functionalization of charcoal powder and as-synthesized carbon nanostructures. The obtained FTIR results are consistent with Raman measurements.

The synthesized samples were further analyzed by XPS to estimate the chemical states of various elements and the presence of functional groups. The survey scan spectra shown in Fig. 5 a and 5d indicated the presence of carbon (C) and oxygen (O) at the surface of the samples C1 and C2, respectively. By analysis of binding energy, the binding nature between C and O in the samples can be confirmed. Considering the case of C1, the high-resolution C1s XPS spectra (Fig. 5b) were deconvoluted into Gaussian peaks centered at 284.5 (C = C, sp2), 284.7 (CC, sp3), 285.5 (CO) 286.3 (COH) and 287.4 (C = O) eV, which confirms the presence of different functional groups [48-49]. The deconvoluted high-resolution O1s XPS spectra (Fig. 5c) containing peaks at 530.7 (O C = O), 531.7 (C = O), 532.6 (COH) and 534.9 (COC) eV also confirm well functionalization of charcoal powder processed at pH 7 [49]. Further, the typical peaks at 283.7 (C = C), 284.3 (CC), 285.2 (CO) and 287.3 eV (COC) in a deconvoluted high resolution C1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 5e) of sample C2 also showing the actuality of various functional groups [48, 50]. The high-resolution deconvoluted XPS spectra of O1s region (Fig. 5f) of sample C2 containing peaks at 530.7 (O C = O), 531.3 (C = O), 532.5 (COH) and 534.8 (COC) further confirms that the charcoal powder processed at pH 9 contains different types of functional groups [49]. Hence, the XPS surveys of samples C1 and C2 further approve the results obtained from Raman and FTIR.

The prepared samples were further analyzed by TG analysis in oxidative air atmosphere. The TG curves of the prepared samples are shown in Fig. 6 . As observed in the TG curves, the charcoal powder (C0) exhibited a high thermal stability up to 400 °C except the slight initial decomposition below 100 °C due to the removal of the moisture in the samples [51]. In the temperature range between 400 and 550 °C, a heavy weight loss of 80% was measured which could be due to the removal of functional groups and combustion of carbonaceous materials. Above 550 °C, the remaining weight was constant up to 650 °C. In contrast, the reduction in mass of sample C3 was observed around 30% up to 450 °C due to removal of water molecules along with oxygen functional groups. The weight loss about 45% is observed between 450 and 525 °C due to carbonaceous materials or volatile compounds induced by oxidative atmosphere [4-5]. No weight reduction in C3 was further found up to 650 °C. The TGA curves of C0 and C3 show that the C0 is comparatively thermally stable between 100 and 400 °C than C3 whereas C3 is highly thermally stable than C0 between 450 and 650 °C. Furthermore, the TGA profiles of C1 and C2 interestingly exhibit better thermal stability than C3 and C0 both. The weight loss of ~ 7% in C1 and ~ 8% in C2 was observed up to 400 °C. Afterwards, the gradual decomposition is continued till 550 °C due to the loss of carbon domains. The weight loss in C2 was more compared to C1 in the temperature range 425–525 °C. The remaining mass was conserved up to 650 °C in both cases. This shows that C1 and C2 becomes more stable than C0 in the entire same range of temperature.

The present work was focused to explore the charcoal as one of the cheapest carbon source for making different types of carbon nanostructures. A facile approach has been developed to prepare CNTs, CNCs and CQDs through a chemical treatment of charcoal powder (which is nothing but nanosheets of carbon) in a mixture of concentrated H2SO4/HNO3 acidic solvent by adjusting pH from 7 to 11 with the help of NaOH solution. The Raman measurements authenticate that all the synthesized samples are graphitic in nature. The TEM measurement provides a better understanding about the formation of CNTs, CNCs and CQDs from charcoal power as a result of varying pH of the reaction medium. The FTIR and XPS results further approve the presence of appropriate bonding composition in the prepared samples. The optical absorption spectra of prepared samples show the different degrees of disruption of π-network due to incorporation of functional groups through chemical treatment. The TG analysis shows the effect of chemical treatment on the thermal stability of the samples. The present discovery could lead to innovative progress in charcoal chemistries.
The corresponding author has designed and formulated the methodology of the present research work. J. Das has made necessary correction in the final draft of the manuscript.
The raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings would be shared on request. .

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no any actual or potential conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: SK is thankful to Science & Engineering Research Board-DST, Govt. of India (File No. PDF/2017/000900) for providing financial support through National Postdoctoral Fellowship (NPDF) scheme.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Considerably improved photovoltaic performance of carbon nanotube-based solar cells using metal oxide layers

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Wang, D. Kozawa, Y. Miyauchi, K. Hiraoka, S. Mouri, Y. Ohno, K. Matsuda, Considerably improved photovoltaic performance of carbon nanotube-based solar cells using metal oxide layers, Nat. Commun.6 (2015) 6305.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of novel carbon nano-chains and their application as supercapacitors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Zhang, N. Zhao, J. Sha, E. Liu, C. Shi, J. Li, C. He, Synthesis of novel carbon nano-chains and their application as supercapacitors, J. Mater. Chem. A2 (2014) 16268–16275.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Engineering triangular carbon quantum dots with unprecedented narrow bandwidth emission for multicolored LEDs

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Yuan, T. Yuan, L. Sui, Z. Wang, Z. Xi, Y. Li, X. Li, L. Fan, Z. Tan, A. Chen, M. Jin, S. Yang, Engineering triangular carbon quantum dots with unprecedented narrow bandwidth emission for multicolored LEDs, Nat. Commun.9 (2018) 2249.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Solvent dependent synthesis of edge-controlled graphene quantum dots with high photoluminescence quantum yield and their application in confocal imaging of cancer cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Rajender, U. Goswami, P.K. Giri, Solvent dependent synthesis of edge-controlled graphene quantum dots with high photoluminescence quantum yield and their application in confocal imaging of cancer cells, J. Colloid Interface Sci. 541 (2019), 387–398.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Oxygen functional groups and electrochemical capacitive behavior of incompletely reduced graphene oxides as a thin-film electrode of supercapacitor

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.J. Oh, J.J. Yoo, Y. Il Kim, J.K. Yoon, H.N. Yoon, J.-.H. Kim, S.B. Park, Oxygen functional groups and electrochemical capacitive behavior of incompletely reduced graphene oxides as a thin-film electrode of supercapacitor, Electrochim. Acta116 (2014) 118–128.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Surface functionalization of graphene quantum dots with small organic molecules from photoluminescence modulation to bioimaging applications: an experimental and theoretical investigation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Qian, J. Ma, X. Shan, L. Shao, J. Zhou, J. Chena, H. Feng, Surface functionalization of graphene quantum dots with small organic molecules from photoluminescence modulation to bioimaging applications: an experimental and theoretical investigation, RSC Adv.3 (2013) 14571–14579.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chemically derived luminescent graphene oxide nanosheets and its sunlight driven photocatalytic activity against methylene blue dye

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Kumar, A. Kumar, Chemically derived luminescent graphene oxide nanosheets and its sunlight driven photocatalytic activity against methylene blue dye, Opt. Mater.62 (2016), 320–327.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tunable (violet to green) emission by high-yield and well dispersed graphene quantum dots and exploiting its unique properties towards sun-light-driven photocatalysis and supercapacitor electrode materials

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Kumar, A.K. Ojha, B. Ahmed, A. Kumar, J. Das, A.
